text: You can chair the meeting well. Now your team is from three countries.
How do I avoid a misunderstanding with colleagues from another culture?

Just to make sure I understand, do you mean the data is final?
I see your point. Perhaps we could look at it differently.
In my culture, we often discuss the details first.
I am happy to adapt to whatever works best for the team.
Perhaps we could look at the data again.
I am afraid that may be a little difficult for us.
Would it be possible to move the deadline?
It might be worth checking with the team in Japan.
To be honest, Patricia, the sample is simply too small. The data is not convincing.
Thank you for the directness, David. Just to make sure I understand, do you mean we need more patients before we publish?
Exactly. And our colleagues in Japan said it may be a little difficult to add patients this quarter.
I see. In their cultural context, that probably means no, so let us not push. To avoid a misunderstanding, I will confirm it with them by email.
Good idea. And please use their family names at first -- it is the local etiquette.
Of course. I will adapt to each team and show some sensitivity. Perhaps we could agree the next steps together.

Welcome to this short session on communicating with global research teams. When you work with colleagues from many countries, the science is the same, but the way people communicate is not. A few simple ideas help you avoid misunderstandings. First, remember that directness changes from culture to culture. In some countries, people say no directly. In others, the answer is softer, such as that may be difficult, and it really means no. So listen carefully to the whole message, not only the words. Second, learn the basic etiquette of each team. In some cultures, people use first names at once. In others, you should use a title and a family name until you are invited to do otherwise. When in doubt, start more formal and adapt later. Third, be aware of cultural norms around time and silence. A pause in a meeting is not always a problem. In some cultures, silence means people are thinking, not that they disagree. Fourth, check that your message is clear. Repeat the key point in different words, and ask a simple question to confirm understanding. Finally, show sensitivity. Assume good intentions, adapt to the context, and never treat one way of communicating as the only correct way. A good global communicator adapts the message to the listener, and turns cultural differences into a strength for the team.

Hi Patricia, this is David from the clinical team. I am calling about the call tomorrow with our colleagues in Japan and Germany about the glycemic control study. I wanted to give you a quick heads-up so we avoid any misunderstanding. Two small things from my side. First, on directness. Our German colleagues are usually very direct and will say exactly what they think about the data, so please do not take it personally. Our Japanese colleagues are often more indirect, so if they say it may be a little difficult, that probably means no, and we should not push too hard. Second, on etiquette. With the team in Japan, please use family names and titles at the start, not first names, until they invite us to do otherwise. It is just a cultural norm, and it shows respect. If you can adapt your message to each group, repeat the key points clearly, and show some sensitivity to the context, the call will go really well. Thanks for leading this, Patricia. Talk soon, and call me back if you want to prepare together before the meeting.

First, learn the basic etiquette of the team, such as whether to use first names or family names. Next, notice how direct people are, because in some cultures no is said in a softer way. After that, adapt your own message to the cultural context of the listener. Then, repeat the key point in different words and confirm understanding. Finally, show sensitivity: assume good intentions and treat every cultural norm with respect.
